item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well this is the second SCP result from googling "sinkhole SCP" (i was looking for SCP-1437 and was extremely disappointed that the images were replaced on it) and boy am i glad i found it.

makes sense that they wouldn't actually let these anamolous people from literal hell reintegrate back into society, of course they'd use them as D-class. there's not enough death row people to make up the insane supply that all these keter-class entities need for containment (i like to imagine 222 is used quite a bit for that, along with 1680 the school bus kid) and this makes a good supply.

I love this. Great characters and worldbuilding, and I love how you've managed to give us a Thaumiel-class that isn't 100% "Safe." This is a good example of a Thaumiel in my opinion, because it expands on the idea of Thaumiel and pushes the boundaries of the definition, without being "OP" or overly bombastic.

(Plus, the idea of the Foundation partnering with Russian hell is both perfect and hilarious.)

My biggest complaint is that I would like more detail/description of the actual torture machines. This is supposed to be Hell, after all. That said, this article is VERY busy as it is, and I feel like you would run the risk of losing your reader if you added anything else at all. So, my complaint is a bit of a moot point!

Other than that, I think some of the Command dialogue is a little too expository, especially in the second log. Obviously the Team members have reviewed the prior footage and received a debriefing — they wouldn't be down there, otherwise. Minor complaint though, and easily lost amid all the other great detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely solid, great work! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good work

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everything up to and the Incursion Log made my vote from neutral to a positive; it's great, keep it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late reply, but they're referring to this forum post and, I assume, mixing up your second-page reply with you being the topic creator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late response, but this is all coming from you skipping over the 17% agnostic number. That, plus the three 1%s (other Christian, Protestant and other) plus the 3% add to 23%.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do love me a good "War on Hell" scenario.

+1

Well, this is a first for me.

I plan to have an addendum for this up fairly soon,1 outlining the mechanics of what exactly a Leibniz-class anomaly is and what other potential examples might look like.

Thank you to everyone.

Footnotes
1. Geologically speaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… think you're confusing me with someone else? I think I managed to figure out what that starred-out phrase was supposed to be, but I've only written two other articles for the site, here and here.

Anyways, glad you enjoyed! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite possibly (though I thought I'd been careful enough). To be honest I was just taken aback at the difference between the two ideas.

Anywho… This is well written but it's still a fairly standard "bug hunt/weird exploration" type article with an admittedly interesting premise.

Neutral vote, but only slightly beneath an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The starred-out phrase really isn't that difficult to parse out. Maybe this would have been better served with a private message?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. I just realised you're the [DATA EXPUNGED] guy.

Nice to see you went down a different road.

Good comeback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you enjoyed it! The ending's intended to be a little open-ended, so read into it what you will… personally, though, I think it's scarier if she isn't being affected at all.

And you're right about all those, thank you. Feexed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that ending. I get what the D-Class in question are, the bigger question is where, specifically, they are coming from.

Spoiler-ish
I warned you

So I can see any of the following scenarios playing out.

1. The D-Class are new instances of -2 that have appeared in the pit and have been collected by the Director of Hell for this exact purpose. Either this means that something recently resulted in a population boom, or they found more of them deeper in the pit.

2. The preceding note, about "reintegrating" is a big fat lie that only exists so the majority of the Foundation doesn't know they are using people from the pit (many of whom are, admittedly, pretty rotten though some are likely falsely accused) and don't want Ethics to show up.

It does, at least, explain why there has been a surprisingly egalitarian mix of D-Class lately when the actual Death Row population is much, much more skewed.

3. They cherry-picked through the -2 population for either those without close matches or the ones who were especially heinous in their previous lives. This seems the least morally ambiguous, but then again Site-667's primary authority figure has the title Director of Hell.

Anyway, I loved reading this. It was engaging beginning to end and didn't have the entire exploration team get murdered in the process. I think that last point is getting a bit stale, so having more than one survivor was refreshing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the "classic" portal-to-hell elements: demons emerging and terrorizing the neighbourhood, herosquads MTFs sent in to explore a doom-esque landscape (glad to see I'm not the only one having this association) with ancient architecture mysteriously embedded in solid rock, monster minions and boss creatures vs. firearms with anti-demon upgrades.

The twist with the truce agreement is almost amusingly pragmatic, but I'm unsure what conclusions to draw from the last messages: Is sending -2 instances as D-replacements in contrast to them being "allowed to reintegrate into society" (as said in the addendum) meant to imply the "Director of Hell" being affected by her "ritually required" title? Maybe I'm just overthinking this…

Here's a couple of possible mistakes I've spotted:

  • Konstantin is misspelled as "Konstatin" a couple of times in the 2nd explog.
  • 3667-1f description wrongly references -1b, which should be -1f itself (the lack of digestive organs…)
  • In the incursion report, oscar is corrected by konstantin, but still says it wrong:
    • Oscar: Right. You’re right. Moles Two and Three, Chi-5 Two, come in. What’s your position?
    • should be:
    • Oscar: Right. You’re right. Moles Two and Three, Chi-5 One, come in. What’s your position?
    • also because C5-One is actually responding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the 2012 Russian Federation poll (Mirny itself didn't have numbers, so I used those from the Sakha Republic):

38% Russian Orthodox, 17% agnostic, 26% atheist, 1% non-Sunni/Shia Islam, 1% non-Orthodox/Catholic/Protestant Christianity, 1% Sunni, 0% non-Russian Orthodox, 13% pagan, 0% Buddhist, 0% Old Orthodox, 1% Protestant, 0% Shia, 0% Catholic, 0% Jewish, 0% Hindu, 0% Pentecostal, 1% other, 3% preferred not to answer.

I'm guessing that all those 0% responses do mask tiny percentages, and it all adds up to over 100% given that some people will profess multiple faiths.

Would it read better if I changed it to "38% Russian Orthodox, 13% pagan, 26% atheist, 23% other"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
;)

I might have some supplemental documentation in the works fleshing out how, exactly, this thing showed up in the first place and who this Guslyakov guy is: stay tuned!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For what it's worth, I read it as more that the Foundation doesn't really WANT to use the 3667-2s as D-class, but a shortage of candidates has led them to pick the least-bad option. I'd presume that normal human D-class are used for the really dangerous stuff, and these poor souls are used for more routine purposes - for example, cleaning 173's cell only requires a willingness to let the janitor die. They're perfect for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have nothing to say other than I loved this, excellent work. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
38% Russian Orthodox, 13% pagan, 26% atheist

What about the other 23%?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the Light of Five Heavens Russian Orthodox Church

Does this connect this to Fifthism, or am I reaching?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, if the foundation doesn't understand how the other SCP works they can't know it won't interact with the way this works.

Secondly, even if other SCPs wouldn't react to the catalyst they might still react badly to a human that can regenerate. What if an effect meant to stop/kill the subject doesn't and as such it keeps intensifying? What if a creature that feeds off of pain finds that it can cause much more pain to one of these subjects without the subject dying, thus giving it a better food source?

None of this is to say that these kind of bad reactions can't happen when testing with regular humans. But if part of the purpose of testing with D-class is to see how the scip reacts to baseline humans, those parts of the results of any experiments these D-class participate in would be null and void.

I might be beating a dead horse again, so I'll stop there. Sorry if I'm a nuisance. IMO this would work better if 3667-2's accelerated healing dropped back to normal as they spend time away from the environment that contains the catalyst.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And those people will show back up in the cavern to be experimented on once more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a fair concern, and I'll admit that part was a little handwaved over in the article.

I intentionally left a 5-year gap between that note and the rest of the investigations: my reasoning was that although the Foundation doesn't understand exactly how the anomalous catalyst operates (otherwise it wouldn't be anomalous), over that time they're at least able to figure out some ground rules for what it can, and can't, affect. They can't be entirely certain it's safe, but they're 99.9% of the way there.

I understand that point can be disputed, though, and I appreciate your point of view on the issue. It was a conclusion I wanted to build to, and I don't believe there's any other way to get there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first you think the poor people who have been pulled out of hell are going to get to live normal lives… then you realize that the Foundation is going to use them as D-Class.

They are literally shipping people out of Hell to fill out the ranks of D-Class.

This skip was fantastic from start to finish; the logs were incredibly polished and immersive. There's not much novelty here (though the final twist/stinger did get a genuine laugh out of me — I knew you were setting something up with that title, but I wasn't expecting that), but everything that is being done is being done extremely well. It's Foundation-lore world-building at its finest.

(Honestly, I'm a little envious; this is precisely the sort of article I have such a hard time writing!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, I must have missed something, why is this Thaumiel again? Didn't it end with the foundation assuming control over the anomalies? I thought the ending was pretty wholesome and satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How could they KNOW there's no cross-contamination problem? The -2's have this catalyst in their blood that the Foundation doesn't understand, who's to say it won't interact with some (demonic) scip in a weird way? No vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my. The ending… and the realisation of why this thing is a Thaumiel (which I'd forgotten by that point). I wasn't expecting a hook, the exploration logs were strong enough that it didn't need one. But then there it was. And here I thought we were gonna have a wholesome ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me a lot of DOOM: a secret militaristic force using demonic (or, in this case, extra dimensional) for either testing or weaponization. I've always loved long scips, and this one might be my favorite dimensional scip.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While stories involving modern disciplined military strength methodically trumping supernatural forces (or DOOM stories, as I like to call them) are borderline overused these days, you somehow managed to make it work with these excellent operation logs. This is simply too well written to dismiss as cliche.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, it reminded me of SCP-517, where the Foundation utilizes military strength against the paranormal as well.
While I love the pictures, I would suggest to censor the faces with black bars. I think that would reinforce the image of a "faceless" Foundation.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This lost some steam by the end but I enjoyed this overall. Competent MTFs that handle a situation appropriately, realistic conversation between them, clever thinking to neutralize a bunch of demons, and many other cool ideas. The portrayal of demons as being intimidating yet really easy to kill was also a nice touch.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it on the forums, loved it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the end, everyone's a bastard, huh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, two competent MTFs march into the Literal Gaping Maw of Death Itself, and instead of jobbing like chumps because 2spooky, they accidentally conquer a suburb of Hell.

I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is nothing short of incredible. It's going to be tough for some to get through, considering it's length, but I was blown away with how immersive and finely-structured the story here was. There's several points I'd like to praise in how it unfolds, but I don't want to spoil things for anyone…

All told, +1 for attention to detail and sheer verisimilitude, +1 for maintaining interest through characterization and atmosphere, +1 for a narrative that had some excellent turns. Love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit rough around the edges at times, but I really respect the ambition and worldbuilding displayed here. The conflicts between the mtf and the entities were honestly quite compelling, and it's always nice to see a competent mtf succeed at their task instead of being lolwrecked to show an anomaly is dangerous. The cocky demon swallowing a grenade in particular was quite amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started out slow, then it started to grow.

It worked for me. The rational technical approach to eliminating eldritch abominations - "yeah, these'r Type III Tartarean entities, you'll want the professional grade antidemonics. I have a couple carboys out in the truck" - contrasted well with the Montague Summers on mescaline imagery.
Poor "Oscar". I hope it was quick.

item.title: Author Post? Hell, Naw!
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes, the good guys win.

Thanks to Athenodora, fieldstone, BananaRepublic, and taylor_itkin for reading this through on the forums, even when I lost your crit! :$

+ show block
– hide block

also i am sorry for increasing the number of Sites by a factor of 10 for fun and profit
